retrieved_docs.title: Periodontitis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The initial layer deposited on the surface of teeth in the formation of dental plaque is "acquired pellicle." [20] This layer is formed within seconds of the exposure of tooth surfaces, followed by the initial attachment of the early colonizers of the biofilm. Streptococcus and Actinomyces species are the primary colonizers which are gram-positive, facultative bacteria. [20] The "adhesin receptors" present on the surface of primary colonizers bind with the proline-rich proteins of the pellicle. This binding leads to revealing of receptor sites known as "cryptitope" which further leads to coaggregation. [21] Gradually there is a layer by layer deposition of dental plaque leading to a deficiency of oxygen in the environment eventually leading to colonization of anaerobic bacteria. [22] The bridging microbe between primary and secondary colonizers is Fusobacterium species. [22] The gradual shift of these aerobic conditions to anaerobic condition marks the progression of gingivitis to periodontitis. Socransky et al. in a study divided the microbes into microbial complexes based on colors. [23] [5] Red and orange complexes bacteria present in the subgingival area are intricately associated with periodontal disease. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Tear Film -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Mucins and glycoproteins secreted by goblet cells also play a role in ocular defense by having decoy receptors for bacteria, thus preventing attachment to ocular tissue, as well as entrapping bacteria or foreign bodies. [1] [5] Furthermore, they concentrate IgA at the mucosal surface where bacteria may be present. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Salivation -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Saliva has many functions which include: Protection Buffering Maintenance of tooth integrity Antimicrobial activity Tissue repair Digestion Assistance with taste.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Salivation -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Saliva functions to protects the oral cavity by several mechanisms, including flushing away pathogens like bacteria, with the help of proteins that have antimicrobial activity like lysozyme, lactoferrin, peroxidase, and small peptides like alpha-defensins and beta-defensins which disrupt the integrity of the microorganisms cellular and mitochondrial functions. Saliva also protects the mucosae of the oral cavity from adhering to each other with the help of the mucinous secretions which provide a lubricating barrier, protecting the mucosa from toxins, trauma, and noxious stimuli. Saliva also acts as a buffer, increasing pH with the help of bicarbonate, phosphate, and other ions. It forms a film called the salivary pellicle which is a binding spot for calcium to help protect the surface of teeth but can also be a binding site for bacteria and increases plaque formation. Biologically active proteins and growth factors are found in saliva and work to regenerate tissue and promote wound healing. Digestion begins in the mouth; saliva contains the enzyme amylase which breaks down starches into maltose and dextrin. This function in itself also reduces the number of sugars available to microorganisms and helps inhibit their growth. Saliva assists with the sensation of taste by solubilizing food so that the taste receptors can interact with the molecules that cause receptor activation. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Salivation -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Acinar cells are the secretory cells of the salivary gland and are either serous or mucinous. These cells differ in their structure and the content that they produce and secrete. Serous acinar cells are spherical and consist of 8 to 12 cells surrounding a central lumen that produce glycoproteins and proteins which function to bind calcium, act as anti-microbial agents, and complete other enzymatic activities. Serous cells have a pyramidal shape with a broad base where the nuclei, rough endoplasmic reticulum, and Golgi apparatus are located and where the secretory granules with the secretory components are formed and stored. The cell units have fingerlike projections that extend into the lumen and increase cell surface area. Mucinous acinar cells contain apomucin, a protein core, with highly substituted sugar residues and acts primarily as a lubricant. The glycoproteins of serous cells are N-linked whereas the mucinous cells are O-linked. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Fungal Infections of the Oral Mucosa -- Pathophysiology -- Oral Candidiasis
retrieved_docs.content: Candida albicans is a commensal fungi in the oral cavity. Commensal microorganisms reside in the external surface of the body or mucosa without affecting human health. However, they may become pathological under appropriate conditions, causing superficial to invasive disseminated infections. Candida reversibly attaches to the oral epithelial cells through electrostatic interactions in its commensal yeast state. [4] Cell-wall receptors like the agglutinin-like sequence family of glycoproteins mediate the attachment. [4] The yeasts change their morphology to filamentous hyphal form whenever the host defense mechanism is impaired, known as phenotypic switching, which is critical for the invasion of the host tissue. [4] Extracellular hydrolytic enzymes like secreted aspartyl proteinases and secreted phospholipases produced by C albicans mediate the destruction of host tissue and subsequent invasion. [4] C albicans can form biofilms on denture bases, increasing virulence and decreasing antifungal susceptibility. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Kingella Kingae -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: K. kingae expresses different structures for successful colonization, carriage, transmission, virulence, and pathogenesis. K. kingae is acquired via direct contact from person to person. Though the mechanism of communication is not well known, there is a high possibility of its spread via droplets. K. kingae produces type IV pili (T4P) under the regulation of rho 54, pilS, and pilR genes. [8] The pili are adhesive proteinous appendages to the respiratory mucosa and synovial membranes. Invasion of the respiratory mucosa by the bacteria is almost always associated with a prior or coincident viral infection [8] . Commonly implicated viruses are human rhinovirus, herpes simplex virus, and herpangina virus, which causes the hand-foot and mouth disease. [8] Invasive K. kingae infection establishes itself via a previous virus-induced mucosal damage in addition to the RTA toxin produced by the organism. RTA toxin also has cytotoxic effects on leukocytes, macrophages, and synovial cells. The polysaccharides capsules on the outer-membrane confer protection against phagocytosis of the bacterial cells. The tendency for K. kingae to produce biofilms assist the pathogen in evading host immunity and defenses. Also, the exopolysaccharides of K. kingae has inhibitory effects on biofilm production from other microorganisms and also facilitates the periodic release of the bacteria cells from biofilms, thereby promoting easier dissemination. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Halitosis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: In physiological halitosis, which occurs on waking, the cause is putrefaction of entrapped food particles and desquamated epithelial cells by bacteria. [9] The surface of the tongue has desquamated epithelial cells, leucocytes from periodontal pockets, food residues, and bacteria. The depth of the tongue papillae affects the biofilm coating on top, which prevents the cleansing action of saliva and promotes the growth of anaerobic bacteria, giving rise to halitosis. This occurs even in individuals with healthy periodontal tissues and good dental hygiene. [26] [14] [15] [16] [27] [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Opsonization -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Opsonization of a pathogen can occur by antibodies or the complement system.

retrieved_docs.title: Streptococcus pneumoniae -- Toxicokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: S. pneumonia has several virulence factors that allow it to cause infections in humans. A polysaccharide capsule interferes with phagocytosis by inhibiting the binding of complement C3b to the cell’s surface. Pneumococcal proteins also play a large role in the virulence of the bacteria. IgA1 protease interferes with host defense at mucosal surfaces, and neuraminidase prevents the attachment to epithelial cells. Other proteins that act in the virulence of S. pneumonia include pneumolysin, pneumococcal surface protein A, and autolysin. Lastly, pili allow for the adherence of the organism to cellular surfaces and play a role in host inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Opsonization -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Opsonization is an immune process which uses opsonins to tag foreign pathogens for elimination by phagocytes. Without an opsonin, such as an antibody, the negatively-charged cell walls of the pathogen and phagocyte repel each other. The pathogen can then avoid destruction and continue to replicate inside the human body.

retrieved_docs.title: Sialolithiasis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The exact pathogenesis of sialolithiasis is not well understood, but two dominant theories were suggested. One of them proposes that there are multiple internal microcalculi within salivary gland secretory granules. When these microcalculi get secreted into the salivary ducts, they may act as a nidus for the formation of larger calculi, ultimately forming a sialolith. [7] The second hypothesis suggests bacteria or food debris within the oral cavity enter the distal submandibular or parotid ducts. Over time, this organic substrate may act as a nidus for the formation of larger calculi. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tooth -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Streptococcus mutans is the most important bacterium associated with dental caries. [3] Bacteria live off the remains of food, especially that of sugar and starches. In the absence of oxygen, these bacteria produce lactic acid, dissolving calcium and phosphorus in the enamel. Demineralization of enamel then leads to tooth destruction. Remineralization of enamel occurs through saliva, which keeps the tooth surface above a critical pH of 5.5. Of concern, however, is that saliva is unable to penetrate through plaque, and demineralization can occur. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Candida auris -- Pathophysiology -- Virulence Factors
retrieved_docs.content: C. auris also form biofilms allowing for adherence to surfaces and plastic materials. Biofilm formation is also strain-dependent, and C. auris shows a reduced ability to adhere and form biofilms compared to Candida albicans due to the rarity of pseudohyphae. [30] [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Integument -- Organ Systems Involved -- Components of the Integumentary System
retrieved_docs.content: Sudoriferous glands, also known as sweat glands, are further divided into eccrine and apocrine glands. Eccrine glands are distributed throughout the body and primarily produce serous fluid to regulate body temperature. [5] Apocrine glands are present in the axilla and pubic area and produce milky protein-rich sweat. [5] These glands are responsible for odor as bacteria break down the secreted organic substances.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Integument -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The final remodeling phase consists of apoptosis as cells are no longer needed and excess structures are broken down in efforts to restore the original architecture. Macrophages secrete matrix metalloproteases that remove excess collagen, and remaining immature collagen matures to finalize the extracellular matrix. [9]

retrieved_docs.content: across pharmaceutical products. Collectively, the community knowledge emphasizes that vitamin A, niacin, and calcium pantothenate are integral components of oral multivitamin‑mineral regimens, often co‑formulated to address diverse micronutrient needs. They serve both active therapeutic functions—cellular support, enzymatic activity, antioxidant protection, phosphate‑chelating and metabolic regulation—and, in certain topical or protein products, inactive excipient roles, underscoring their importance in nutritional supplementation, metabolic health, and patient‑care strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 578
retrieved_docs.content: multivitamin and mineral products listed for niacin, often together with the same suite of vitamins and minerals, and may be accompanied by inactive excipients such as miconazole, salicylic acid, chlorhexidine gluconate, or other formulation agents. A contraindication is recorded for pantothenát vápenatý in patients with or at risk for kidney stones (حصاة في الكلية), reflecting the relevance of its calcium‑based structure and phosphate‑chelating properties to stone formation. Additionally, a topical extracorporeal liquid formulation containing miconazole, salicylic acid and chlorhexidine gluconate lists niacin as an inactive ingredient, highlighting an inconsistency with its predominant role as an active vitamin in oral supplements. Overall, the community data emphasize that both niacin and calcium pantothenate are common, active constituents of comprehensive micronutrient preparations, contribute to essential metabolic pathways, and must be considered in the context of specific

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 185
retrieved_docs.content: in non‑oral products (e.g., SUN PREMIUM FACE 50 SPF, the extracorporeal liquid containing miconazole, salicylic acid and chlorhexidine); its pharmacologic effect is relevant only when administered systemically. Clinically, calcium pantothenate is contraindicated in patients prone to calcium‑related renal calculi because its calcium content may exacerbate stone formation, necessitating renal risk assessment before recommending high‑dose calcium‑containing multivitamins. Collectively, vitamin A, niacin (vitamin B3) and calcium pantothenate (vitamin B5) are integral components of diverse multivitamin and mineral supplements, each providing distinct biochemical functions—antioxidant and cellular modulation (vitamin A), metabolic and cardiovascular support (niacin), and co‑enzyme A‑dependent energy metabolism (calcium pantothenate). Their frequent co‑presence with other vitamins (C, D, E, B‑group) and minerals underscores a comprehensive strategy to address multiple micronutrient needs

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 178
retrieved_docs.content: are extensively utilized across diverse dosage forms (oral tablets, capsules, chewables, sublingual solutions, powders and topical preparations) as active agents that modulate cellular metabolism, enzyme activity and antioxidant defenses, or as excipients that contribute to product stability. Their frequent co‑presence in multivitamin and mineral supplements reflects an integrated strategy to provide comprehensive micronutrient support for preventive health, metabolic function and overall nutritional adequacy, while calcium pantothenate requires caution in patients prone to renal calculi.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 36
retrieved_docs.content: and specialized nutraceutical formulations. Vitamin A provides retinoid‑mediated vision and epithelial health; niacin delivers B‑vitamin‑mediated metabolic and neuro‑protective effects; calcium pantothenate drives co‑enzyme A‑dependent metabolism through its phosphate‑chelating activity. Their combined actions enhance antioxidant capacity, immune function and growth, contributing to comprehensive patient care. Clinicians should note that calcium pantothenate is contraindicated in individuals with a history of nephrolithiasis (kidney stones), and products containing this compound should be avoided in such patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: products. In these products calcium pantothenate is co‑formulated with the same suite of B‑vitamins, vitamins A, C, D, E, a wide array of trace minerals (iron, zinc, copper, magnesium, manganese, selenium, chromium, molybdenum, iodine), omega‑3 fatty acids, and probiotic strains. Its physiological effect is described as “cellular activity alteration” via its role as a precursor of coenzyme A, supporting numerous enzyme‑driven metabolic pathways essential for energy production and biosynthesis. Calcium pantothenate is classified in the ATC system under “other plain vitamin preparations,” reflecting its status as a single‑nutrient supplement, and chemically belongs to the calcium‑compound class. Beyond nutrition, it exhibits a phosphate‑chelating mechanism of action and may be used as an inactive ingredient or excipient in non‑vitamin products such as a topical sunscreen cream (containing oxybenzone, octinoxate, avobenzone, titanium dioxide), a miconazole/salicylic‑acid/chlorhexidine

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1711
retrieved_docs.content: Pantothenát vápenatý, the calcium salt of pantothenic acid, is classified as a member of the vitamin B complex and belongs to the structural class of calcium compounds. It is widely used as an active ingredient in oral multivitamin and mineral preparations, appearing in formulations that combine it with vitamins A, C, B1 (thiamine), B2 (riboflavin), B3 (niacin), B6 (pyridoxine), B9 (folic acid), B12 (cyanocobalamin), biotin, iron, magnesium, zinc, copper, selenium, chromium, alpha‑lipoic acid, lutein and other nutrients. In addition to its role as an active component, pantothenát vápenatý can be present as an inactive ingredient in topical or extracorporeal liquids that contain agents such as miconazole, salicylic acid and chlorhexidine gluconate. Physiologically, pantothenát vápenatý exerts a cellular activity‑altering effect, reflecting its participation in coenzyme A synthesis and related metabolic pathways. Its mechanism of action includes phosphate‑chelating activity, which may influence mineral balance and cellular signaling. Because of its calcium content and chelating properties, pantothenát vápenatý is contraindicated in individuals with kidney stones, and caution is advised when it is considered for patients at risk of nephrolithiasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 303
retrieved_docs.content: behavior and its conversion to active retinaldehyde and retinoic acid metabolites. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate that vitamin A is a versatile compound integrated across oral supplements, fortified foods, and topical preparations, where it functions chiefly as an active nutrient but can also serve ancillary roles in product matrices. Understanding its diverse applications, mechanisms of action, and structural classification is essential for clinicians and formulators aiming to optimize patient outcomes and product efficacy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1773
retrieved_docs.content: in a powder containing cyanocobalamin, riboflavin, pantothenic acid, niacin, thiamine, and pyridoxine, as well as in a topical extracorporeal liquid that includes miconazole, salicylic acid, and chlorhexidine gluconate. This dual classification reflects differences in formulation intent: in multivitamin capsules it serves as a source of vitamin B5, whereas in other products it may function as a filler or stabilizer. Chemically, pantothenát vápenatý belongs to the structural class of calcium compounds, indicating that the calcium ion is coordinated with the pantothenate anion. Its pharmacologic actions include alteration of cellular activity, consistent with the role of vitamin B5 in coenzyme A synthesis and energy metabolism, and a phosphate‑chelating activity that can influence mineral balance and biochemical pathways involving phosphorylated intermediates. Because of its calcium content and potential to affect phosphate handling, pantothenát vápenatý is contraindicated in

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 30
retrieved_docs.content: and formulation stability. Clinically, calcium pantothenate is contraindicated in patients prone to kidney‑stone formation. Collectively, these nutrients illustrate an integrated micronutrient supplementation strategy: vitamin A, vitamin E and niacin are co‑formulated to boost antioxidant capacity and visual health; niacin and calcium pantothenate, as members of the vitamin B complex, provide metabolic support, energy production and tissue‑repair functions; and the extensive inclusion of these vitamins across oral, topical and fortified products underscores their multifaceted physiological roles in preventive health, disease management and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 186
retrieved_docs.content: topical/extracorporeal liquids (e.g., miconazole‑salicylic‑acid gels). Its inclusion supports metabolic health, lipid regulation and overall nutritional adequacy. Calcium pantothenate (the calcium salt of pantothenic acid, vitamin B5) functions as a precursor of coenzyme A, enabling phosphate‑chelating enzymatic reactions essential for energy metabolism, fatty‑acid, cholesterol and neurotransmitter synthesis. It is listed as an active ingredient in multivitamin‑iron products, prenatal supplements and specialty vitamin complexes, frequently combined with iron sources (ferrous fumarate, ferrous bisglycinate), vitamin C forms (ascorbic acid, sodium ascorbate), other B‑vitamins (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, pyridoxine, biotin, cyanocobalamin, folic acid, levomefolate) and essential minerals (calcium, magnesium, manganese, zinc, copper, selenium, chromium, iodine). Calcium pantothenate also serves as an inactive excipient in diverse topical and cosmetic formulations, including

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 304
retrieved_docs.content: 12 g in 26 g ORAL POWDER, FOR SOLUTION [Nourish]”. It contributes to coenzyme A synthesis and overall metabolic function and is chemically classified as a calcium‑based, phosphate‑chelating substance. Calcium pantothenate also occurs as an inactive ingredient in the miconazole‑salicylic‑chlorhexidine liquid preparation. Clinical guidance notes that calcium pantothenate is contraindicated in patients with a history of kidney stones, underscoring the need for caution when recommending supplements containing this vitamin. Together, these data illustrate that both niacin and calcium pantothenate are integral components of comprehensive multivitamin‑mineral regimens, frequently combined with a wide array of other micronutrients to support metabolic health, and that their inclusion—whether as active nutrients or excipients—must be considered in the context of individual patient conditions such as nephrolithiasis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 173
retrieved_docs.content: ingredient in SUN PREMIUM FACE 50 SPF topical cream and in the Nourish whey‑protein hydrolysate powder. Calcium pantothenate functions as a co‑factor in numerous enzymatic pathways, supporting co‑enzyme A synthesis and the metabolism of carbohydrates, proteins and fats; its physiological effect is described as “cellular activity alteration,” reflecting influence on cellular energy production and biosynthetic processes. Its phosphate‑chelating activity underlies its role as a calcium‑based compound. Clinically, calcium pantothenate is contraindicated in individuals with a history of nephrolithiasis, reflecting the risk that excess calcium may promote kidney‑stone formation. Collectively, these summaries illustrate that vitamin A, niacin (vitamin B3) and calcium pantothenate (vitamin B5) are frequently co‑present in oral multivitamin and mineral products, often alongside other vitamins, minerals, specialty nutrients, probiotics and omega‑3 fatty acids to achieve nutritional adequacy,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 204
retrieved_docs.content: also appears as an inactive ingredient in topical or extracorporeal liquids containing miconazole, salicylic acid, chlorhexidine gluconate, in the sunscreen SUN PREMIUM FACE 50 SPF, in whey‑protein hydrolysate powder Nourish, and in hydrocortisone 10 mg/mL cream. Clinically, it is contraindicated in patients with renal calculi and should be used with caution in individuals predisposed to nephrolithiasis; acylcarnitine levels are inversely related to the vitamin B complex. Vitamin D (cholecalciferol) is a frequent component of multivitamin tablets, iron‑multivitamin liquids and calcium‑enriched preparations. Its primary mechanism is inhibition of bone resorption, thereby supporting skeletal health and reducing the risk of osteoporosis. Consequently, vitamin D is often combined with calcium pantothenate, calcium, iron, zinc, selenium and other micronutrients to promote both cellular metabolism and bone integrity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 100

Niacin also serves as an inactive component in topical antimicrobial/keratolytic preparations (e.g., Miconazole 0.02 %/Salicylic Acid 0.02 %/Chlorhexidine Gluconate). Pantothenic acid (vitamin B5) is biochemically linked to niacin, and its calcium salt, calcium pantothenate, is a frequent active ingredient in the same multivitamin products. Calcium pantothenate functions as a nutritional co‑factor in co‑enzyme A‑dependent metabolic pathways and as a cicatrizant (wound‑healing agent) in the ATC system, where it is classified both as an “other plain vitamin preparation” and an “other cicatrizant.” Its mechanism involves participation in co‑enzyme A metabolism and phosphate‑chelating activity, offering potential benefits in osteoporosis prevention while being contraindicated in patients prone to kidney stones. Calcium pantothenate (typically 5–7 mg) is listed in products such as Folivane‑OB,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 125

Calcium pantothenate is contraindicated in patients with kidney stones, indicating the need for caution in this population. Vitamin D is a common component of multivitamin tablets and iron/multivitamin liquids and is linked to calcium oyster shell. It inhibits bone resorption, is directly associated with osteoporosis, and therefore plays a critical role in skeletal health and calcium metabolism. Collectively, vitamin A (including retinol acetate), niacin, calcium pantothenate, and vitamin D are integral constituents of diverse pharmaceutical, nutraceutical, and cosmetic products. They may act as active therapeutic agents or supporting excipients, participate in cellular and enzymatic processes, and address broad nutritional needs ranging from antioxidant protection and metabolic regulation to
